title: Pointers and Arrays in C
description: How pointers store addresses, how they connect to arrays, and how to use both together.
congratsText: You mastered Pointers and Arrays in C!

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
Objectives
  • Explain what a pointer stores, using & and *
  • Show that an array's name is the address of its first element
  • Change a value by writing through a pointer
  • Pass a pointer to a function to change the caller's data

sections.title: What Is a Pointer?
sections.lesson:
What Is a Pointer?
Every variable lives at an address in memory, like a house on a street. A normal variable's house holds a value, like 10. A pointer is different — it holds the address of another variable's house, not a value.
int x = 10; int *p = &x;
Think of p as a sticky note with x's house address written on it. p itself is not 10 — you read the note, walk to that address, and 10 is what you find there. That "walk over and look inside" step is called dereferencing.

quiz.question: What does a pointer variable actually store?
options: A separate copy of another variable's value
The memory address of another variable
The default value of another variable
The size in bytes of another variable

sections.title: Declaring and Using a Pointer
sections.lesson:
Declaring and Using a Pointer
& asks "what's this variable's address?" * means something different depending on where it shows up.
* in a declaration
"This variable will hold an address." int *p means "p is a pointer to an int."
* anywhere else
"Go to this address and get the value there." This is called dereferencing.
int x = 10; int *p = &x; printf("%d", *p); // 10 *p = 20; printf("%d", x); // 20
p's note still points at x's house. Writing through *p changes what's inside that house, so x changes too — there's only one house, not two.

quiz.question: p points at x. What does `int y = *p;` do?
options: Copies x's current value into y
Makes y point at the same address as p
Changes x to equal y
Causes a compile error since p is a pointer

sections.title: Pointers and Arrays
sections.lesson:
Pointers and Arrays
An array is a row of houses built right next to each other, with no gaps. An array's name, in most expressions, decays into the address of the first house in the row.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; int *p = scores; // same as &scores[0] printf("%d", *p); // 90
scores
The whole row of 5 houses. sizeof(scores) counts all 5.
p
One sticky note holding one address. sizeof(p) only counts the note.
scores never becomes a real pointer variable with its own note — it's just always understood as "the address of house #1."

quiz.question: Why does sizeof(scores) give a different answer than sizeof(p) when p = scores?
options: sizeof always rounds arrays up to the nearest pointer size
p secretly points to a smaller copy of the array
scores is the whole array; p is only one pointer variable
scores stores its address twice, p only once

sections.title: Changing a Value Through a Pointer
sections.lesson:
Changing a Value Through a Pointer
p points at scores[0]. Writing through *p reaches into that same house and changes what's inside — there's only one house, so scores[0] changes too.
int *p = scores; // p -> scores[0] (90) *p = 100; printf("%d", scores[0]); // 100
*p = 100;
Follow the note, walk to the house, change what's inside. scores[0] becomes 100.
p = 100;
Scribble a new (invalid) address on the note itself. scores[0] never gets touched.
The star is what tells C "go to the house," not "change the note."

quiz.question: p points at scores[0], which holds 90. After *p = 100;, what does scores[0] hold now?
options: 90, unchanged
It becomes a pointer itself
The program will not compile

sections.title: How Big Is a Pointer?
sections.lesson:
How Big Is a Pointer?
A house address is written the same size on paper whether the house is small or huge. In the same way, a pointer's size never depends on what it points to — it only has to hold one address.
printf("%lu", sizeof(int)); // 4 printf("%lu", sizeof(int*)); // 8 (typical 64-bit) printf("%lu", sizeof(double*)); // 8
An int* and a double* take up the same space on the sticky note, even though an int house and a double house are different sizes inside.

quiz.question: On a 64-bit system, why is sizeof(int*) the same as sizeof(double*)?
options: int and double are secretly the same size on 64-bit systems
The compiler rounds all sizeof results up to 8 bytes
Pointers to double always store two addresses instead of one
Both pointers only store an address, regardless of type

sections.title: Passing a Pointer to a Function
sections.lesson:
Passing a Pointer to a Function
Normally, calling a function in C hands it a photocopy of your variable — it can scribble on the copy, but your original stays untouched. Passing an address instead hands over the real house's location, so the function can walk over and change the original.
void addTen(int *n) { *n = *n + 10; } int score = 90; addTen(&score); printf("%d", score); // 100
addTen() never receives score itself, only directions to its house. Following those directions with *n reaches the real variable back in main.

quiz.question: Why does addTen() need & at the call site, but its parameter is declared as int *?
options: & and * mean the same thing in C
& turns a variable into its address, matching the int * parameter
& only works with arrays, not plain variables
int * tells C to ignore whatever is passed in

sections.title: Pointer Pitfalls and Best Practices
sections.lesson:
Pointer Pitfalls and Best Practices
An uninitialized pointer isn't a blank sticky note — it already has some leftover address scribbled on it from whatever used that memory before. Follow that scribble, and there's no telling which house you'll end up at.
int *p; // dangerous: p holds a leftover scribble printf("%d", *p); // undefined behavior
  • Write a real address on every pointer's note right away, or set it to NULL
  • Check for NULL before following a pointer that might not have a real address yet
  • Never follow a pointer to a house that's already been torn down

quiz.question: What is the actual danger of `int *p;` followed by `printf("%d", *p);`?
options: p is automatically set to 0, so *p always prints 0
The compiler blocks this code from running at all
p points at itself, creating an infinite loop
p holds an unknown address, so *p reads unpredictable memory

sections.title: NULL and Pointers to Pointers
sections.lesson:
NULL and Pointers to Pointers
NULL is a sticky note that plainly says "no address yet — don't follow me." It's the safe default until a pointer has somewhere real to point.
A pointer can also point at another pointer. int **pp is a note that says "go find another note" — that second note (p) is the one that finally points at the real house.
int x = 10; int *p = &x; int **pp = &p; printf("%d", **pp); // 10 — follow two notes to get there
This is handy when a function needs to change which address a pointer holds, not just the value at that address — it needs a note pointing at the note.

quiz.question: In `int **pp = &p;`, what does pp store?
options: The value that p points to
A copy of p's address, but for a different variable
The address of the pointer variable p itself
The address of x, skipping p entirely

sections.title: Recap
sections.lesson:
Recap
  • A pointer is a sticky note holding another variable's address, not its value
  • * follows the note to read or write the real value; & writes the address on a new note
  • An array name is always the address of its first house, but isn't a pointer variable itself
  • Writing through *p changes the real variable; assigning to p itself just rewrites the note
  • A pointer's own size never changes — it's always just one address
  • Passing an address to a function lets it change the caller's real variable
  • An uninitialized pointer holds a leftover scribble — always give it a real address or NULL
  • A pointer to a pointer is a note that leads you to another note
